title: Personal Information
shortSummary: Talk about who you are: your name, nationality, job, and where you live.
culturalNote: When stating your profession, French drops the article: Je suis professeur (I am a teacher), with no 'un'. Nationalities are adjectives and agree with gender: il est français, elle est française. The French value a certain politeness and discretion, so deep personal questions usually come later in a relationship; job and city are normal small talk.

grammar.explanation: When saying what you do, French drops the indefinite article: Je suis médecin (I am a doctor), not Je suis un médecin. The same applies to nationalities and religions.
grammar.commonMistake: Adding un or une: it is Je suis professeur, not Je suis un professeur.

grammar.explanation: Use venir de to say where you are from and habiter à to say where you live: Je viens d'Espagne, mais j'habite à Paris.
grammar.commonMistake: Mixing them up. Origin uses venir de; current home uses habiter à.

dialogue.setup: At a language exchange, Tom introduces himself to Sophie.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.native: Bonjour, je m'appelle Tom. Et vous ?
lines.english: Hi, my name is Tom. And you?

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.english: My name is Sophie. Nice to meet you!

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.native: Je viens d'Espagne, mais j'habite à Paris.
lines.english: I'm from Spain, but I live in Paris.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.english: I'm a student. I study computer science.

quiz.question: How do you say 'I come from Australia'?
quiz.explanation: Origin uses venir de, elided to d' before a vowel.

quiz.question: J'_____ à Paris. (I live in Paris)
quiz.explanation: habiter means to live; j'habite is the je form, elided.

quiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
quiz.explanation: French drops the article before professions.

quiz.question: Which word means 'married'?
quiz.explanation: marié means married; célibataire means single.

quiz.question: Arrange: Sophie / m'appelle / je
quiz.explanation: Je m'appelle (I call myself) plus the name.
